edges.description: Enzyme deficiency causes lysosomal accumulation of undegraded glycopeptides.
Substrate accumulation drives progressive infantile neurodegeneration.

nodes.description: Biallelic NAGA variants (e.g., the E325K substitution) abolish lysosomal alpha-N-acetylgalactosaminidase activity, the hydrolase that removes terminal alpha-N-acetylgalactosamine residues from glycopeptides and glycolipids.

evidence.reference_title: Schindler disease: the molecular lesion in the alpha-N-acetylgalactosaminidase gene that causes an infantile neuroaxonal dystrophy.
evidence.snippet: Schindler disease is a recently recognized infantile neuroaxonal dystrophy resulting from the deficient activity of the lysosomal hydrolase

nodes.description: Failure to cleave terminal alpha-N-acetylgalactosamine causes accumulation of O-linked glycopeptides and blood-group-A glycoconjugates, with marked urinary glycopeptide excretion.

evidence.reference_title: Structural and immunocytochemical studies on alpha-N-acetylgalactosaminidase deficiency (Schindler/Kanzaki disease).
evidence.snippet: a wide spectrum including an early onset neuroaxonal dystrophy (Schindler disease) and late onset angiokeratoma corporis diffusum (Kanzaki disease)

evidence.reference_title: Schindler disease: the molecular lesion in the alpha-N-acetylgalactosaminidase gene that causes an infantile neuroaxonal dystrophy.
evidence.snippet: a glutamic acid to lysine substitution in residue 325 (E325K)

nodes.description: Progressive neuronal dysfunction produces an infantile neuroaxonal dystrophy with axonal spheroids in grey matter and rapidly progressive neurodegeneration.

evidence.reference_title: Schindler disease: an inherited neuroaxonal dystrophy due to alpha-N-acetylgalactosaminidase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Axonal 'spheroids' were observed histologically in the grey matter
